old_claim.note: Telescope provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Orbital observatories and later space telescopes extend telescope lineage, while the 1960 TIROS imaging anchor does not require a telescope for every optical payload.

source_shape.source_locator: NASA traces space-telescope concepts to Lyman Spitzer's 1946 argument for an extraterrestrial observatory and says a telescope in orbit escapes atmospheric blurring and distortion.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Space telescopes are a mature branch of spaceborne optical instruments, extending older telescope lineage above the atmosphere.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The telescope source supports lineage for the observatory branch, but the corrected 1960 node also covers Earth-imaging optical cameras, so historical_predecessor is more precise than a hard enabling edge.

ontology_before: Space-Based Advanced Optics treated telescope as a generic enabling prerequisite for every spaceborne optical payload.
ontology_after: Spaceborne Optical Instruments keeps telescope as lineage for orbital observatories while allowing camera and spectrometer payloads to remain inside the broader node.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed edge type: telescope is now historical_predecessor rather than enabling.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed only to space telescopes and excludes cameras, radiometers, and spectrometers.
A stronger source shows the 1960 anchor was a telescope rather than television camera imaging.
Space_telescopes is split so telescope lineage belongs only on that narrower downstream node.
short_reason: Telescope is lineage for one branch, not the component required by every scoped payload.
